Unity Software Inc.。为其财务引擎增添了大量燃料，因为又一次强劲的软件IPO正在进行中。
据一位知情人士透露，这家旧金山公司生产设计师用来创建视频游戏的软件引擎，周四晚间将首次公开募股(IPO)定价为每股52美元，高于已经很高的目标区间。Unity U，-0.46%将带来至少13亿美元的收入，按这个价格计算，估值为137亿美元，预计将于周五上午在纽约证券交易所(New York Stock Exchange)开始交易，股票代码为U。
Unity提供了它的游戏引擎-一个软件系统，它提供了一个框架，开发者可以在其中构建他们的游戏，而不必向开发者重新发明轮子，而且只有在超过一定的收入里程碑后才开始收取许可费。
“Unity对独立开发者非常有吸引力，”Forrester研究总监Chris Gardner告诉MarketWatch。
Unity于2004年在丹麦推出，名为Over The Edge Entertainment，2009年迁往美国并更名，当时它吸引了红杉资本(Sequoia Capital)550万美元的投资。自那以后，Unity十多年来一直保持私有状态，截至2019年7月，持续的几轮融资将其在私人市场的估值推高至60亿美元。该公司去年亏损1.632亿美元，营收为5.418亿美元，销售额增长42.2%，亏损同比增长24%。
Unity上市之际，其最大的竞争对手Epic Games(Youku Inc.)正面临着与两大视频游戏平台的竞争，这两个平台分别是苹果公司(Apple Inc.)的iOS和字母表公司(Alphabet Inc.)的谷歌(Google)安卓系统。EPIC以其广受欢迎的“堡垒之夜”游戏和联合竞争对手虚幻引擎而闻名，在苹果和Alphabet的谷歌各自的应用商店被启动后，EPIC起诉苹果和谷歌公开试图绕过他们的支付系统，避免向他们支付“堡垒之夜”的收益分成。苹果还试图启动虚幻引擎，这本来可以阻止开发者使用该软件，但法官阻止了这种情况的发生，同时允许“堡垒之夜”继续被禁止进入苹果的应用商店。
Unity也在一波炙手可热的软件IPO浪潮中上市。雪花公司。本周早些时候，Snow-10.39%创下软件公司有记录以来最大规模的IPO，JFrog Inc.也加入了这一行列。Frog，-0.50%和相扑逻辑公司(Sumo Logic Inc.)。相扑，-3.00%。这三家公司在华尔街的首日交易都表现强劲，雪花公司(Snowflake)的股价上涨了一倍多，JFrog的股价上涨了近50%，相扑逻辑公司(Sumo Logic)的股价在本周的头几个交易日跃升了20%以上。
Unity计划在此次发行中出售至少2500万股，以高盛(Goldman Sachs)和瑞士信贷(Credit Suisse)为首的承销银行可以额外获得380万股，这可能会推高总发行量。
note_en:
Unity Software Inc. is adding a lot of fuel to its financial engine, as yet another strong software IPO gets underway.
  The San Francisco company, which makes a software engine that designers use to create videogames, priced its initial public offering at $52 a share Thursday evening, according to a person familiar with the matter, higher than  an already elevated target range. Unity  U,  -0.46% stands to bring in at least $1.3 billion at a valuation of $13.7 billion at that price, and is expected to begin trading Friday morning on the New York Stock Exchange under the ticker symbol U.
  Unity offers its gaming engine — a software system that provides a framework in which developers can build their games without having to reinvent the wheel — free to developers, and only starts charging a licensing fee after a certain revenue milestone has been crossed.
  “Unity is very attractive to independent developers,” Forrester research director Chris Gardner told MarketWatch.
  Launched in Denmark in 2004 as Over the Edge Entertainment, Unity moved to the U.S. and changed its name in 2009, when it attracted $5.5 million in investment from Sequoia Capital. Unity has stayed private for more than a decade since, with continuing funding rounds moving its valuation up to $6 billion on the private markets as of July 2019. The company lost $163.2 million last year on revenue of $541.8 million, with sales growing 42.2% and losses increasing by 24% from the year before.
    Unity is going public as its biggest competitor, Epic Games Inc., faces a fight with two of the biggest platforms for videogames, Apple Inc.’s  AAPL,  -1.59% iOS and Alphabet Inc.’s  GOOGL,  -1.65%  GOOG,  -1.66% Android. Epic, known for its popular “Fortnite” game as well as the Unity rival Unreal Engine, has sued Apple and Alphabet’s Google after being booted from their respective app stores for openly attempting to bypass their payment systems and avoid paying them a cut of “Fortnite’s” proceeds. Apple also attempted to boot the Unreal Engine, which could have blocked developers from using the software, but a judge barred that from happening, while allowing “Fortnite” to remain banned from Apple’s App Store.
    Unity also goes public amid  a wave of hot software IPOs. Snowflake Inc.  SNOW,  -10.39%  recorded the biggest IPO for a software company on record earlier in the week, and has been joined by JFrog Inc.  FROG,  -0.50% and Sumo Logic Inc.  SUMO,  -3.00% . All three enjoyed strong debuts on Wall Street, with Snowflake more than doubling, JFrog increasing nearly 50% and Sumo Logic jumping more than 20% in their respective first days of trading this week.
     Unity planned to sell at least 25 million shares in the offering, and underwriting banks — led by Goldman Sachs and Credit Suisse — have access to an additional 3.8 million shares that could push the total take higher.
title: 市场观察

我经常得到这样的问题，为什么新冠肺炎从哪里来很重要？我能看出两个主要原因。一个是我们需要知道以防止未来的大流行。如果SARS-CoV-2病毒从蝙蝠跳到中间宿主再到人类，那么我们想知道我们能知道的关于这个过程的一切。
溢出效应不是随机发生的。人类和特定种类的动物，通常是哺乳动物或鸟类，以恰到好处的方式接触，使病毒能够从一个物种跳到另一个物种。
病毒暂时从动物传染给人类是很常见的，但通常情况下，当病毒从动物传染给人类时，它们并不善于从人传染给人类。我们需要了解病毒成功进化到人与人之间传播的情况。
在SARS-CoV-2的案例中，有很多问题。云南的矿工/农民/其他人是否经常接触该病毒的亲属？在2020年前，有多少人口的SARS冠状病毒抗体检测呈阳性？
SARS-CoV-2可能会感染哪些动物？猫和水貂都可以感染&很可能在它们之间传播；还有其他动物也是这样吗？
在武汉之前，SARS-CoV-2在人与人之间传播的可能性有多大？我们现在和将来如何能够检测到这一点呢？各国是否应该就共享有关新发流行病的数据达成协议？
SARS-CoV-2被直接改造的可能性有多大？是通过实验室动物还是通过组织培养？我们怎么知道呢？是否存在或是否存在安全程序，以防止在没有审计跟踪的情况下进行功能研究？
考虑到各自的政治动机，中国、美国和世界其他国家如何在调查大流行源头方面进行合作？或者，如果现在不是，如何建立制度来激励他们/使他们在未来能够合作？
我在核安全领域观察到，政治领导人经常希望缓和局势，避免双方都面临代价极其高昂的战争的风险，但他们觉得自己的合作能力受到政治动机的限制，特别是他们的基础希望他们看起来强硬。
这就引出了我认为弄清楚新冠肺炎大流行的起源很重要的第二个原因。作为一个全球文明，我们需要找出一个“过程”来调查潜在的和实际的大灾难，并找出预防这些灾难的方法。
基本上，我们在对每个国家都重要的问题上缺乏强有力的合作方法。我们需要在和平时期建立的系统，在危机期间和之后对紧张的政治环境具有强大的抵抗力。
了解新冠肺炎是如何发生的，是为以后的工作进行实践，因为那里的赌注可能会更高。例如，大规模的地球工程项目需要更大的能力才能在困难的科学问题上达成共识。
随着人口的增长和技术的进步，我们可以做更多的事情。我们的力量水平正在提高，但我们无法预测我们力量增强的影响…。
例如，如果你踏上一台时光机，带着SARS-CoV-2的基因组前往2018年…。如果病毒在没有实际释放的情况下在人群中传播，没有人能够预测它会做什么。
你可以尝试用动物模型来研究它的传播性，然后分别使用不同的动物模型来研究疾病的严重程度，但这两种方法都很困难，答案也不准确。不管病毒是天然的还是人工制造的，这一点都是正确的。
人类种群本质上是病原体的生态系统，我们根本没有建模能力来了解新物种在生态系统中的表现。当与可能具有异常良好适应性的有机体配对时，这是危险的。
这在比喻上和字面上都是正确的。打个比方，物种是一种新技术，生态系统是人类社会。引入以算法为媒介的社交媒体，你就无法预测会发生什么，无论是它的增长速度还是一、二阶效应。
无论是一种新的病毒还是一种新的西兰花，我们可能创造的新有机体都是如此。这并不是说，当谈到非常新的东西的影响时，我们“没有”预测能力。一种新的西兰花不太可能破坏它的环境。
一个新的物种/技术越强大/越适合，就越难确定该物种/技术的影响。
note_en:
I often get the question, "why does it matter where COVID-19 came from?"  I can see two main reasons. One is we need to know to prevent future pandemics. If SARS-CoV-2 jumped from a bat to an intermediate host to a human, then we want to know everything we can about that process
 Spillovers don't happen randomly. Humans and specific kind of animals, usually mammals or birds, come into contact in ways just right for a virus to be able to jump from one species to another.
 It's common for viruses to jump from animals to humans temporarily, but usually when they do they aren't good at transmitting from human to human. We need to understand the circumstances where viruses successfully evolve human to human transmission.
 In the case of SARS-CoV-2, there are a lot of questions. Were miners / farmers / others regularly exposed to relatives of the virus in Yunnan? How much of the population before 2020 tested positive for antibodies to SARS-like coronaviruses?
 What kinds of animals might SARS-CoV-2 be able to infect? Cats and mink can be infected & probably transmit among themselves; are there other animals for which this is true?
 How likely is it that SARS-CoV-2 was spreading person to person before Wuhan? How would we be able to detect that, now and in the future? Should countries make agreements with each other to share data about emerging pandemics?
 How likely is SARS-CoV-2 to be directly engineered? Passaged through lab animals or tissue culture? How would we know? Are there or can there be safety procedures in place that would prevent gain of function research from being done without an audit trail?
 How can China, the US, and the rest of the world cooperate on the investigation of pandemic origins given their respective political incentives? Or if not now, how could systems be set up to incentivize them / enable them to cooperate in the future?
 I've observed in the nuclear security world that political leaders often want to deescalate and avoid the risk of an extremely costly war to both sides but feel limited in their ability to cooperate by their political incentives, specifically their base wanting them to look tough
 This brings me to the second reason I think figuring out the origins of the COVID-19 pandemic is important. We, as a global civilization, need to figure out a *process* for investigating big potential and actual catastrophes and a method for preventing these
 Basically, we lack robust methods of collaboration around issues important to every country. We need systems set in place during "peacetime" that will be robust to tenser political environments during and after a crisis.
 The work to understand how COVID-19 happened is practice for future work where the stakes may be even higher. For example, largescale geoengineering projects would require a much greater capacity to reach consensus on difficult scientific questions
 As the population grows and technology improves, we can do a lot more. Our power level is improving, but we cannot anticipate the effects of the our power increasing...
 For example, if you stepped in a time machine and went to 2018 with the genome of SARS-CoV-2... No one would be able to predict what the virus would do if it were to spread among the human population without actually releasing
 You might try to use animal models to study its transmissibility, and separately use different animal models to study the disease severity, but both would be difficult and the answers would not be precise. This is true regardless of whether the virus was natural or engineered.
 Human populations are essentially ecosystems for pathogens, and we lack the modeling ability to understand much at all about how a new species will fare in an ecosystem. This is dangerous when paired with organisms that might have unusually good fitness.
 This is both metaphorically and literally true. As a metaphor, a species is a new technology and the ecosystems are human societies. Introduce algorithmically mediated social media and you can't predict what will happen, either its rate of growth nor first & second order effects
 And it's literally true of new organisms we might create, whether this is a novel virus or a new kind of broccoli. This isn't to say we have *no* predictive power when it comes to the effects of something very new. A new broccoli is unlikely to damage its environment.
 The the more powerful / fit a new species / technology is, the harder it will be to determine the effects of that species / technology.
title: 为什么COVID从哪里来很重要
title_en: Why it matters where COVID came from

note:
光疗法可以帮助提升情绪，治愈伤口，增强免疫系统。它也能改善帕金森氏症的症状吗？计划于今年秋天在法国启动的首个此类试验旨在找出答案。在七名患者中，植入他们大脑的光缆将把近红外(NIR)光脉冲直接传送到黑质，黑质是大脑深处的一个区域，在帕金森氏症中会退化。这个团队由Clinatec研究所的神经外科医生Alim-Louis Benabid领导，Clinatec研究所是几个政府资助的研究机构和工业界的合作伙伴关系，他们希望光线能保护那里的细胞不会死亡。
这项研究是探索帕金森氏症患者如何从光线中受益的几项研究之一。“我太兴奋了，”佛罗里达大学医学院的神经心理学家道恩·鲍尔斯(Dawn Bowers)说，他正在招募病人参加一项试验，在这项试验中，近红外线将被照射到颅骨中，而不是通过植入物输送。
在帕金森氏症患者和动物模型中进行的小型试验已经表明了其益处，但一些帕金森氏症主流研究人员对此表示怀疑。没有人确切地表明光可以如何保护关键的神经元，或者为什么它应该对埋藏在大脑深处的从未见过光的细胞产生任何影响。怀疑者说，到目前为止在人们身上看到的许多或所有鼓舞人心的暗示可能是安慰剂效应的结果。哥伦比亚大学欧文医学中心的神经生物学家大卫·苏尔泽(David Sulzer)是NPJ帕金森病杂志的编辑，他说，因为没有生物标志物与帕金森症状的变化有很好的相关性，“我们依赖于观察行为”。“要防范安慰剂效应并非易事.”
但支持者指出，帕金森氏症疗法名为脑深部刺激(DBS)，在这种疗法中，特定频率的电流被施加到受影响的大脑区域。DBS由Benabid在30多年前发明，已成为治疗帕金森患者震颤和其他严重运动症状的标准方法，尽管其作用模式也不完全清楚。马萨诸塞州总医院韦尔曼光医学中心的研究员迈克尔·汉布林(Michael Hamblin)说，记录在案的低强度激光疗法对其他组织的愈合效果也令人鼓舞。在一些国家，医生经常使用激光来治疗疼痛或加速伤口愈合。
十年前，悉尼大学的神经解剖学家约翰·米特罗凡尼斯(John Mitrofan Is)受到启发，尝试在帕金森氏症中使用光线，因为他的一位同事告诉他，近红外范围内的光线可以保护视网膜细胞免受毒素的伤害。在2012年的一项研究中，他和他的同事在帕金森氏症的小鼠模型中表明，近红外光照射到小鼠的头部可以保护黑质中产生多巴胺的细胞免受神经毒素的伤害。
米特罗凡尼斯很兴奋，打电话给贝纳比德，他曾和贝纳比德一起学习星展银行一年。“作为外科医生，贝纳比德说，‘我们必须开发一种接近该区域的照明设备，’”米特罗凡尼斯回忆道。研究人员推断，从头骨外部照射出来的光线不会穿透到足够深的地方，不会对较大的动物产生影响。
2017年，他们与研究员塞西尔·莫罗(Cécile Moro)一起，给20只猕猴注射了一种已知会导致帕金森症状的神经毒素。在其中的9个人中，他们还通过植入的设备将近红外发射到了中脑区。米特罗凡尼斯回忆起第一只接受近红外治疗的猴子在恢复期3周后的表现：“他四处走动，好像没有什么问题一样。我们看着对方，只是拥抱了一下。…。这是令人欣喜若狂的。“。总体而言，接受近红外线治疗的猴子比未接受治疗的猴子出现的症状较少，并且多保留了20%到60%的神经毒素靶向的脑细胞。
米特罗凡尼斯还与凯瑟琳·汉密尔顿(Catherine Hamilton)展开了合作，后者是塔斯马尼亚州的一名退休职业医生，曾用发光二极管(LED)包裹自己的关节炎膝盖。在去年发表的一项针对6名帕金森氏症患者的研究中，汉密尔顿、米特罗凡尼斯和其他人报告说，戴上镶有LED的头盔可以改善面部表情、听觉处理、参与对话、睡眠质量和动力，尽管它对运动症状没有太大影响。居住在澳大利亚朗福德的帕金森氏症患者艾伦·明森(Alan Minson)从2019年7月开始使用头盔，他说：“如果我错过了一天的疗程，我的身体会逐渐发生变化。”“噩梦又回来了，我的耐力水平大大下降，昏昏欲睡的情况也越来越严重。”悉尼大学的安·利伯特(Ann Liebert)正计划对120名使用更复杂头盔的患者进行研究。在一项类似的努力中，Bowers将随机选择24名患者接受外部应用的近红外或假光，并观察行为和运动方面的好处。
鲍尔斯还将寻找一些迹象，如一些人所建议的那样，光线可以促进脑细胞产生能量的线粒体。试管实验表明，光可以触发存在于线粒体膜上的细胞色素C氧化酶，以加速细胞能量的产生，这反过来可能会增加血液流动，刺激细胞大量产生几种神经保护蛋白和生长因子。鲍尔斯说：“但我不相信经颅设备能穿透到足够深的地方，显示出实质性的改善。”她对贝纳比德的审判更有希望。
这项研究将对14名早期帕金森氏症患者进行为期4年的跟踪调查，其中7名患者将通过一根细激光二极管电缆定期接受670纳米光的脉冲治疗。其他七名患者将不会接受手术；道德审查委员会裁定，不能在没有机会受益的情况下让他们接受手术。Benabid说，主要目标是证明植入物是安全的，但研究人员还将评估疾病进展情况。“这必须带来很大的不同，”他说。“没有理由为了轻微的改善而(做)广泛的手术。”
研究人员计划使用常见的成像方法来量化患者体内产生多巴胺的细胞数量。但是，可能很难检测到保护作用。苏尔泽说：“帕金森氏病的所有神经保护试验的主要问题是，诊断似乎发生在超过50%的多巴胺产生细胞消失之后。”除非改进是巨大的，否则“信号可能太小，检测不到。”
该团队还将寻找临床益处。但由于研究人员通过观察患者执行特定任务来对帕金森的症状进行评级，评估在很大程度上是主观的，症状随着时间的推移而变化；苏尔泽说，每个人都有好日子和糟糕的日子。由于对照组不会接受手术，因此特别难排除安慰剂效应。
然而，苏尔泽对像贝纳比德这样的研究持怀疑态度。他说，缺乏明确的机制并不是放弃治疗的理由。“有很多事情我们都不明白，”苏尔泽说。“我对此表示怀疑，也认为这是一个耐人寻味的领域。”
note_en:
Light therapy can help lift moods, heal wounds, and boost the immune system. Can it improve symptoms of Parkinson’s disease, too? A first-of-its-kind trial scheduled to launch this fall in France aims to find out. In seven patients, a fiber optic cable implanted in their brain will deliver pulses of near-infrared (NIR) light directly to the substantia nigra, a region deep in the brain that degenerates in Parkinson’s disease. The team, led by neurosurgeon Alim- Louis Benabid of the Clinatec Institute—a partnership between several government-funded research institutes and industry—hopes the light will protect cells there from dying.
 The study is one of several set to explore how Parkinson’s patients might benefit from light. “I am so excited,” says neuropsychologist Dawn Bowers of the University of Florida College of Medicine, who is recruiting patients for a trial in which NIR will be beamed into the skull instead of delivered with an implant.
 Small tests in people with Parkinson’s and animal models of the disease have already suggested benefits, but some mainstream Parkinson’s researchers are skeptical. No one has shown exactly how light might protect the key neurons—or why it should have any effect at all on cells buried deep in the brain that never see the light of day. Much or all of the encouraging hints seen so far in people may be the result of the placebo effect, skeptics say. Because there are no biomarkers that correlate well with changes in Parkinson’s symptoms, “we are reliant on observing behavior,” says neurobiologist David Sulzer of Columbia University Irving Medical Center, an editor of the journal  npj Parkinson’s Disease. “It’s not easy to guard against placebo effects.”
 But proponents point to a Parkinson’s therapy named deep brain stimulation (DBS), in which electricity of a specific frequency is applied to affected brain regions. Invented by Benabid more than 30 years ago, DBS has become a standard approach for treating tremors and other severe motor symptoms in Parkinson’s patients even though its mode of action isn’t entirely clear either. The well-documented healing effect of low-level laser therapy on other tissues is also encouraging, says Michael Hamblin, a researcher at the Wellman Center for Photomedicine at Massachusetts General Hospital. In some countries, doctors routinely use lasers to treat pain or speed up wound healing.
 Ten years ago, John Mitrofanis, a neuro-anatomist at the University of Sydney, was inspired to try light in Parkinson’s after a colleague told him that light in the NIR range protected retinal cells against toxins. In a 2012 study, he and colleagues showed in a mouse model of Parkinson’s that NIR light shined into mice’s heads protected dopamine-producing cells in the substantia nigra from a neurotoxin.
 Excited, Mitrofanis called Benabid, with whom he once spent a year studying DBS. Benabid, “being the surgeon, said, ‘We have to develop a light device that gets close to the area,’” Mitrofanis recalls. The researchers reasoned that light shining from outside the skull would not penetrate deep enough to make a difference in larger animals.
 In 2017, together with research fellow Cécile Moro, they injected 20 macaques with a neurotoxin known to cause Parkinson’s symptoms. In nine of them, they also delivered NIR to the midbrain area through an implanted device. Mitrofanis recalls how the first NIR-treated monkey behaved after a 3-week recovery period: “He was moving around like there was nothing wrong. We looked at each other and just hugged. … It was euphoric.” Overall, NIR-treated monkeys developed fewer symptoms than the untreated group and retained 20% to 60% more of the brain cells targeted by the neurotoxin.
 Mitrofanis also struck up a collaboration with Catherine Hamilton, a retired occupational physician in Tasmania who had treated her own arthritic knee by wrapping it with light-emitting diodes (LEDs). In a study of six Parkinson’s patients published last year, Hamilton, Mitrofanis, and others reported that wearing a helmet lined with LEDs improved facial expression, auditory processing, engagement in conversation, sleep quality, and motivation, though it did not have much effect on motor symptoms. “If I miss a day session, there is a gradual change in me,” says Alan Minson, a Parkinson’s patient living in Longford, Australia, who started to use a helmet in July 2019. “Bad dreams come back, my tolerance level goes way down, and my lethargy goes way up.” Ann Liebert of the University of Sydney is planning a study in 120 patients using a more sophisticated helmet. In a similar effort, Bowers will randomize 24 patients to externally applied NIR or sham light and watch for behavioral and motor benefits.
 Bowers will also look for signs that, as some have proposed, light boosts brain cells’ energy-producing mitochondria. Test tube experiments have shown that light can trigger the enzyme cytochrome C oxidase, which is present on mitochondrial membranes, to rev up cellular energy production, which in turn might increase blood flow and stimulate cells to churn out several neuroprotective proteins and growth factors. “But I’m not convinced a transcranial device can penetrate deeply enough to show substantial improvements,” Bowers says. She’s more hopeful about Benabid’s trial.
 That study will follow 14 early-stage Parkinson’s patients for 4 years, seven of whom will be treated periodically with pulses of 670-nanometer light delivered to the brain via a thin laser diode cable. The other seven patients will not be operated on; an ethical review board ruled against subjecting them to surgery without a chance of benefit. The main objective is to prove the implant is safe, Benabid says, but the researchers will also evaluate disease progression. “It has to make a big difference,” he says. “There is no reason [to do] extensive surgery for mild improvement.”
 The researchers plan to use common imaging methods to quantify the number of dopamine-producing cells in patients. But a protective effect may be hard to detect. “The major problem with all neuroprotection trials in Parkinson’s disease is that the diagnosis appears to occur after more than 50% of dopamine-producing cells are gone,” Sulzer says. Unless the improvement is huge, “the signal might be too small to detect.”
 The team will also look for clinical benefits. But because researchers grade Parkinson’s symptoms by observing patients performing specific tasks, assessments are largely subjective, and symptoms vary over time; everyone has good days and bad days, Sulzer says. Because the control group will not undergo surgery, it will be especially hard to rule out placebo effects.
 Yet Sulzer is giving studies like Benabid’s the benefit of the doubt. The absence of a clear mechanism isn’t a reason to dismiss the therapy, he says. “There are many things we don’t understand,” Sulzer says. “I am skeptical and also think it is an intriguing area of pursuit.”
title: 治疗帕金森氏症的新武器开始试验：光
title_en: Trials begin for a new weapon against Parkinson’s: light

note:
我站在我的餐厅里，周围都是玩具和当晚剩下的晚餐，然后我就站在了脚底。厨房里，一袋垃圾被翻倒了，里面散落在地板上。在起居室里，一周的衣物已经等了两周才叠好。
当时是上午9点45分，我已经筋疲力尽了。我会在前一天晚上午夜回家，结果却不得不在早上5点醒来。和我蹒跚学步的儿子在一起，然后带他和他的妹妹去学校，然后为清洁女工打扫卫生。我有四个小时的托儿时间和两个故事要写。我丈夫在上班；我在这里收拾烂摊子。
我33岁，是两个孩子的母亲，筋疲力尽。我不想让洗衣服和做家务成为我的余生。我不想总是沉浸在工作、照看孩子、打扫房间和晚餐中，承受着劳动的首当其冲。在过去的两年里，我一直在乞求帮助照顾孩子和做家务，结果却被告知，如果这些都太多了，我可以辞去工作。“不算太多，”我说了一遍又一遍。“这并不是我的全部工作。”
十个月前，我签署了我的第一份图书合同。我在33岁生日时签了合同，梦想成真。我花了11年时间写了三部失败的小说。一本失败的回忆录。我参加了一个MFA项目，然后写了一本书的提案，但没有卖出去。
所以我们有了孩子，我花了五年时间做全职母亲，在我生活的缝隙中写作-深夜和清晨。我曾试图找一份工作，但在爱荷华州的锡达拉皮兹，作家的工作很难找到，而且我做了所有的工作-校对员、跆拳道杂志的编辑、提案撰稿人、自由专栏作家、营销文案、大学作文导师、继续教育教师向经济衰退期间下岗的人教授Word和Excel。
2005年，我们为了我丈夫梦寐以求的工作搬到了锡达拉皮兹，我们的计划一直是，最终，我们会为我搬家。但是每一年过去了，我们再也没有这样做过。一旦我们有了孩子，尽管他的工作很灵活，他的老板也很乐于助人，但我意识到这永远不会轮到我。因此，我开始像疯子一样做自由撰稿人：深夜给编辑发推介，接受采访，而我那爱抱怨的蹒跚学步的孩子在公园里追着我，在他身后留下了一串水果小吃。我会抄写采访，在录音的背景中听到好奇的乔治的回声。
后来我得到了一些署名，然后，经过多年的工作，在2016年，一切都改变了。我写的一篇文章引起了一家大学出版社的注意，他们想要一本书。几个月后，另一家出版社的一位编辑对我的作品印象深刻，建议根据我为Jezebel网站写的关于母亲和神话的系列写一本书。
那会有更多的工作，我知道的。但我们可以让它行得通。他已经实现了他的梦想。现在我要实现我的梦想。我需要的只是一个小小的帮助。拿到它比我预想的要难。
我们的故事并不新鲜。这是美国每一对异性恋夫妇的故事。从统计数据来看，妇女要做更多的育儿和家务工作，当我们用来填补缺口的所有安全网都没有了时，这种情况在大流行中只会变得更糟。不再有清洁工或保姆了。不再有托儿所，甚至不再上师范学校。不再有年迈的父母帮助我们。只有我们和我们之间日益扩大的平等鸿沟。
最后，我开始在“偷来的朋友圈”写第一本书。拼凑保姆的钱意味着要掏出微不足道的自由职业者支票。策划研究旅行感觉就像是创造了一台鲁布·戈德堡(Rube Goldberg)机器-提前准备冷冻餐，安排额外的保姆，朋友，也许可以从外地来一位奶奶？我的好朋友梅尔负责舞蹈独奏会的彩排，带着头发和化妆完成了彩排，并给我发了一段练习的视频。不知何故，仍然没有干净的衣物。所以我做了职场女性一直做的事：我更多地外包。我雇了一名房屋清洁工，尽管我丈夫提出了抗议。
直到我得到了一份住院医生的工作，我才能写出那本书的大部分内容，这会给我所需的休息时间。我不在的时候，我儿子得了行走肺炎，在儿童博物馆摔倒割破了嘴唇。我的家人说我太自私了。我需要和我的孩子们在一起。“他们有父亲了！”我喊道，“他怎么了？”我三十天写了七万字，因为我知道如果我当时不写，我永远也写不出来。就在下个月，我站在那个布满玩具的餐厅里，在压力下崩溃了。然后我搬出去了。
离婚后，我有第二本书要写。这是我写的第一本书，当时我正在管理我的家庭责任中不成比例的一部分。但是第二次我就按照我自己的方式写了。我不需要讨价还价，因为我们有50-50的监护权。我也不必说服别人让我外包家庭清洁。最后我根本不需要雇任何人，因为我的房子干净多了。在重新谈判我的生活时，我为自己谈判了一个更好的协议，这是法院下令的。我不再乞求把照看孩子或打理家务的部分负担转嫁给我的丈夫。我们的监护权协议规定他和我平分财产。
当然，这仍然很难。我仍然一边写这本书，一边忙于全职工作和账单，以及即将出版的另一本书，但这一次没有冷冻餐，没有清洁工，没有用餐期望，洗衣也更少。
我开始开玩笑说，离婚才使我的婚姻平等。但就像所有好笑的笑话一样，这只是因为它是真的才好笑。
在美国，在异性关系中，女性所做的工作是男性的三倍。诚然，现代男性比以前做得更多，但他们的努力并不接近晚上的天平。当他们真的捐钱的时候，他们期待的不仅仅是信用，还有慷慨的赞扬。尽管他们增加了家务活的份额，但他们报告做的比实际做的要多。
在这场流行病中，这种分裂正在粉碎美国母亲。在双方都工作的异性伴侣中，女性现在多做70%的育儿工作。母亲和父亲都在家--如果有工作的话，他们都表面上在工作，照看孩子，监督在线学校--女性平均比男性多做15个小时的工作。
怎样才能使婚姻最终平等呢？(离婚并不是每个人都可以选择的！例如，想要保持婚姻关系的人。)。我永远不会通过婚姻治疗来找到平等-当然，我不得不设立并发出预约提醒-或者通过不断地就家务进行谈判。保姆(我们负担不起)或房屋清洁工(我们能做到，但勉强能做到)永远不会做到这一点。
莱兹·伦茨是“贝拉博：孕妇和上帝土地权利的辩护”一书的作者。她是“雪松急流公报”的专栏作家，和她的两个孩子住在爱荷华州。
note_en:
I stood in my dining room surrounded by toys and the remnants of dinner from the night before sticking to the bottom of my feet. In the kitchen, a bag of trash was tipped over, its innards strewn across the floor. In the living room, a week’s worth of laundry had been waiting two weeks to be folded.
 It was 9:45 a.m., and already I was exhausted. I’d come home at midnight the night before, only to have to wake up at 5 a.m. with my toddler son and then take him and his sister to school, before cleaning for the cleaning lady. I had four hours of childcare and two stories to write. My husband was at work; I was here with the mess.
  I was 33, a mother of two, and bone-tired. I didn’t want the laundry and chores to be the rest of my life. I didn’t want to always be drowning in work and childcare and housecleaning and dinner, bearing the brunt of the labor. I’d spent the past two years begging for help with the kids and housework, only to be told that I could just quit my job if it was all too much. “It’s not too much,” I’d said over and over. “It’s just not all my job.”
      Ten months before, I’d signed my first book contract. I signed the contract on my 33rd birthday, and it was a dream come true. I had spent 11 years writing three failed novels. One failed memoir. I’d attended an MFA program and then wrote a book proposal that hadn’t sold.
  So we had children and I spent five years as a  stay-at-home mother, writing in the crevices of my life—the late nights and early mornings. I had tried to find a job, but in Cedar Rapids, Iowa, jobs for writers were hard to find and plus, I’d done them all—proofreader, editor of a tae kwon do magazine, proposal writer, freelance columnist, marketing copywriter, college composition instructor, continuing education instructor teaching Word and Excel to people laid off during the recession.
 We’d moved to Cedar Rapids for my husband’s dream job in 2005, and the plan was always that, eventually, we’d move for me. But each year passed and we never did. Once we had kids, even though his job was flexible and his boss was accommodating, I realized it would never be my turn. So I began freelancing like a maniac: sending late-night pitches to editors, conducting interviews while my whining toddler chased me around the park, leaving a trail of fruit snacks in his wake. I’d transcribe interviews and hear the echoes of  Curious George in the background of the recordings.
 Later I got some bylines and then, after years of work, in 2016, everything changed. An article I wrote got the attention of a university press; they wanted a book. Months later an editor at a different publishing house had been impressed by my writing and suggested a book based on a series I had written for the website Jezebel about motherhood and mythology.
  It would be a lot more work; I knew that. But we could make it work. He had achieved his dream. Now I would achieve mine. What I needed was just a little help. Getting it was harder than I’d expected.
 Ours was not a new story. It’s the story of every heterosexual couple in America. Statistically,  women do more childcare and more housework, and it’s only  getting worse in a pandemic, when all the safety nets we used to fill in the gaps are gone. No more house cleaners or nannies. No more daycare or  even normal school. No more aging parents helping us. Nothing but us and the yawning gap in equality between us.
  In the end I started writing the first book in stolen moments. Cobbling together money for babysitters meant wringing out paltry freelance checks. Orchestrating research trips felt like creating a Rube Goldberg machine—prepare freezer meals in advance, line up extra sitters, friends, maybe a grandma could come from out of town? My good friend Mel handled the dance recital’s dress rehearsal, complete with hair and makeup, and sent me a video of the practice. Somehow there was still never clean laundry. So I did what working women have done forever: I outsourced more. I hired a house cleaner, although my husband protested.
 I didn’t get to write the bulk of that book until I landed a residency that would give me the break I needed. While I was gone, my son had walking pneumonia and fell and cut his lip at the Children’s Museum. My family told me I was being selfish. I needed to be with my children. “They have a father!” I shouted, “What about him?” I wrote 70,000 words in 30 days, because I knew if I didn’t write it then, I’d never be able to do it. The very next month, I stood in that toy-strewn dining room, cracking under the pressure. Then I moved out.
  After the divorce, I had a second book to write. The first I had written while managing a disproportionate share of my family’s domestic responsibilities. But the second I wrote on my own terms. I didn’t have to bargain for child-free hours because we had 50-50 custody. I also didn’t have to convince someone else to let me outsource household cleaning. In the end I didn’t need to hire anyone at all, because my house was cleaner. In renegotiating my life, I had negotiated a better deal for myself, and it was court-ordered. I no longer begged to shift even some of the burden of childcare or housekeeping onto my husband. Our custody agreement mandated that he and I bear an equal share.
  It was, of course, still hard. I still wrote the book while juggling full-time work and bills and the imminent release of  another book, but this time there were no freezer meals, no house cleaner, no dinner expectations, and less laundry.
 I began to joke that it had taken a divorce to make my marriage equal. But like all good jokes, it’s only funny because it’s true.
 In America, in heterosexual relationships, women do three times as much work as their male counterparts. It’s true that modern men are doing more than they once did, but their efforts do not come close to evening the scales. When they do chip in, they expect not just credit, but  lavish praise. And though they have increased their share of household chores, they  report doing more than they  actually do.
 In the pandemic, this split is shattering American mothers. Among heterosexual couples where both partners work, women are now doing  70% more childcare. With mothers and fathers at home—both of them ostensibly working, if they’re employed, watching children, and supervising online school—women are doing on average  15 hours more work than men.
 What would it take to make a marriage finally equal? (Divorce isn’t an option for everyone! Like, for example, people who want to stay married.) I was never going to find equality through marriage therapy—which, naturally, I had to set up and send reminders for appointments for—or through constant negotiating over chores. It was never going to be achieved by nannies (which we couldn’t afford) or house cleaners (which we could, but barely).
  Lyz Lenz is the author of  Belabored: A Vindication of the Rights of Pregnant Women  and  God Land.  She’s a columnist for the Cedar Rapids Gazette  and lives in Iowa with her two kids.
title: 离婚才让我的婚姻变得平等
title_en: It Took Divorce to Make My Marriage Equal

该公司周三将区间从34美元至42美元上调至44美元至48美元。
Unity首席执行官约翰·里奇蒂洛(John Riccitiello)和首席财务官金伯利·贾巴尔(Kimberly Jabal)采取了与典型IPO不同的方式，从投资银行手中控制了定价和分配。
CNBC证实，视频游戏软件开发商Unity周四的IPO定价高于预期区间，最初对该公司的估值为137亿美元。
Unity在周三将区间提高至44美元至48美元后，以每股52美元的价格出售股票。路透社(Reuters)最先报道了定价。Unity发言人没有立即回复记者的置评请求。
Unity正在结束今年最繁忙的IPO周之一，雪花公司(Snowflake)周三的首次公开募股(IPO)突显了这一点，该公司市值翻了一番以上，收盘市值超过700亿美元。
成立于2004年的Unity在过去十年里已经成为游戏创作的主要参与者，它为开发者提供了为手机、游戏机和网络创建3D标题的工具，而不必为每个平台编写代码。该公司在招股说明书中表示，它拥有150万月度活跃的创建者，使用其软件的开发者每月的下载量超过30亿次。
精灵宝可梦Go和钢铁侠VR都是使用Unity软件开发的游戏。它也用于艺电(Electronic Arts)、Take-Two Interactive、腾讯和育碧发行的游戏。
2020年上半年的收入同比增长39%，达到3.513亿美元，消费10万美元或更多的客户数量从515人增加到716人。Unity&39；今年前六个月的净亏损从一年前的6710万美元收窄至5410万美元。
CNBC本周早些时候报道，曾执掌艺电的Unity首席执行官约翰·里奇蒂洛(John Riccitiello)在确定IPO价格时采取了一种非传统的方法。与2019年加入公司的首席财务官金伯利·贾巴尔(Kimberly Jabal)一起，里奇蒂洛接管了定价和分配的控制权，而不是依靠投资银行做出最终决定。
投资者提交了对一定价格和他们想要的股票数量的出价。然后，Unity团队将根据出价落地的地点选择价格，任何提交低于该价格的投资者都不会获得股票。对于那些出价达到或高于最终价格的公司，Unity高管将决定分配。
note_en:
The company raised its range on Wednesday to $44 to $48 from $34 to $42.
 Unity CEO John Riccitiello and CFO Kimberly Jabal took a different approach from typical IPOs by taking control of the pricing and allocation from the investment banks.
 Video game software developer  Unity priced its IPO above the expected range on Thursday in an offering that initially values the company at $13.7 billion, CNBC has confirmed.
 Unity sold shares at $52 apiece, after  lifting the range on Wednesday to between $44 and $48. Reuters was first to report on the pricing. A Unity spokesperson didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.
  Unity is wrapping up one of the  busiest weeks for IPOs this year, highlighted by the Wednesday debut of  Snowflake, which  more than doubled in value to close the day with a market cap over $70 billion.
 Founded in 2004, Unity has become a major player in game creation over the past decade by giving developers the tools to create 3D titles for phones, consoles and the web without having to code for each platform. The company said in its prospectus that it has 1.5 million monthly active creators and that developers using its software are seeing over 3 billion downloads a month.
 "Pokemon Go" and "Iron man VR" are among the games developed using Unity's software. It's also used for games published by  Electronic Arts,  Take-Two Interactive,  Tencent and  Ubisoft.
 Revenue in the first half of 2020 rose 39% from a year earlier to $351.3 million and the number of customers spending $100,000 or more increased to 716 from 515. Unity's net loss in the first six months of the year narrowed to $54.1 million from $67.1 million a year ago.
 Unity CEO John Riccitiello, who previously ran EA, took a nontraditional approach toward determining the price of the IPO,  CNBC reported earlier this week. Along with CFO Kimberly Jabal, who joined the company in 2019, Riccitiello assumed control on the pricing and allocation, rather than leaning on investment banks to make the final call.
  Investors submitted their bids for a certain price and the number of shares they wanted. The Unity team would then choose the price based on where the bids landed, and any investors who submitted an offer below that price wouldn't receive shares. For those that put in bids at or above the final price, Unity executives would decide on the allocation.
title: Unity将首次公开募股(IPO)定价为每股52美元，高于预期区间，这将使该公司的初始估值达到137亿美元
title_en: Unity has priced its IPO at $52 per share, above the expected range, that would initially value the company at $13.7B
